content:
What is EC2?
EC2 stands for Elastic Compute Cloud.
Forget the full name. Here’s what it actually means:
EC2 is a virtual computer you rent from AWS.
Just like your laptop — it has a CPU, RAM, storage, and an operating system. The difference? It runs in Amazon’s data center. Not on your desk.
Why is this useful?
Your laptop turns off. Your EC2 instance stays on 24/7. Your laptop is in one location. Your EC2 instance is on the internet — accessible from everywhere. Your laptop has limited resources. EC2 can scale from tiny to massive instantly.
This is why every web application, every API, every real product runs on servers like EC2.

Key EC2 Concepts
Before we launch — understand these 4 things:
1. Instance
An instance is one virtual computer. When you “launch an EC2 instance” — you’re starting one virtual computer in the cloud.
2. Instance Type
This determines how powerful your computer is.
Instance Type CPU RAM Use for t2.micro 1 vCPU 1GB Learning, small projects (FREE TIER) t2.small 1 vCPU 2GB Small applications t2.medium 2 vCPU 4GB Medium applications t3.large 2 vCPU 8GB Production applications
Start with t2.micro — it’s free tier eligible.
3. AMI (Amazon Machine Image)
This is the operating system for your instance.
Think of it like choosing Windows vs Mac vs Linux for your laptop.
For beginners — choose Ubuntu Server 22.04 LTS. It’s free, widely used, and has the most tutorials available.
4. Key Pair
This is how you securely connect to your instance.
AWS generates a special file (called a .pem file) that acts like a password. You download it once and use it every time you connect.
Never lose this file. Never share it. Never upload it to GitHub.

Launch Your First EC2 Instance — Step by Step
Step 1 — Open EC2 Console

• Go to AWS Console

• Make sure region is Mumbai (ap-south-1) — top right corner

• Search “EC2” in the search bar

• Click EC2
Step 2 — Click Launch Instance
You’ll see a big orange button — “Launch instance”
Click it.
Step 3 — Name Your Instance
Give it a simple name. Example: “my-first-server”
Step 4 — Choose Your AMI
Scroll down to “Application and OS Images”
Search for “Ubuntu” → Select “Ubuntu Server 22.04 LTS”
Make sure it says “Free tier eligible”
Step 5 — Choose Instance Type
Select t2.micro
You’ll see “Free tier eligible” next to it.
Step 6 — Create a Key Pair
Click “Create new key pair”

• Key pair name: “my-first-key”

• Key pair type: RSA

• Private key file format: .pem
Click “Create key pair”
A .pem file will download automatically. Save this file somewhere safe. You need it to connect.
Step 7 — Configure Security Group
This controls what traffic can reach your server.
Click “Edit” next to Network settings.
Make sure these rules exist:

• SSH — Port 22 — Source: My IP (for connecting to your server)

• HTTP — Port 80 — Source: Anywhere (for web traffic)
Step 8 — Launch
Scroll down. Click “Launch instance”
Wait 1–2 minutes.
Your server is now running in Amazon’s Mumbai data center.

Connect to Your Instance
Find Your Public IP
Go to EC2 → Instances → Click your instance
You’ll see “Public IPv4 address” — something like 13.233.45.67
Copy this IP.
Connect from Your Computer
Open your terminal and run:
# First — fix the key file permissions
chmod 400 my-first-key.pem# Connect to your server
ssh -i "my-first-key.pem" ubuntu@YOUR-PUBLIC-IPReplace YOUR-PUBLIC-IP with the IP you copied.
You’ll see a message asking if you trust this connection. Type yes and press Enter.
You’re now INSIDE your cloud server.
Welcome to Ubuntu 22.04.3 LTS (GNU/Linux 5.15.0-1031-aws x86_64)
ubuntu@ip-172-31-XX-XX:~$That prompt means you’re connected. You’re in.

Common Errors and Fixes
Error 1 — Permission denied (publickey)
Permission denied (publickey)Fix: You’re using the wrong key file. Make sure you’re using the exact .pem file you downloaded for this instance.
Error 2 — Connection timed out
ssh: connect to host xx.xx.xx.xx port 22: Connection timed outFix: Your security group doesn’t allow SSH from your IP. Go to Security Groups → Edit inbound rules → Add SSH rule with your current IP.
Error 3 — Unprotected private key file
WARNING: UNPROTECTED PRIVATE KEY FILE!Fix: Run chmod 400 your-key.pem to fix file permissions.
Error 4 — Instance not reachable after stopping and starting
Cause: Public IP changes every time you stop/start an instance. Fix: Get the new public IP from the console each time. Or use Elastic IP for a permanent IP address.
